actions.thought: 95o is a very weak and unconnected offsuit hand. Even though I am on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand lacks the playability and equity to justify opening. Folding to conserve chips is the standard and optimal play.

In a 3-handed game, 8To in the Small Blind is a reasonable hand to play when the Button folds. While I could raise to steal the blinds, completing the bet (calling) allows me to see a flop cheaply without building a large pot out of position against the Big Blind. I will call the additional 50 chips to see the flop.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Three offsuit. The small blind has completed to match the big blind, bringing the pot to 200. Since I have already posted the big blind of 100, checking is the correct action to see a flop with a speculative hand against the small blind in position.

I have bottom pair on a board with two overcards (K and Q) and a potential flush draw. There's no need to build a large pot with a weak made hand. Checking allows me to see how the Big Blind reacts and potentially get to a cheap showdown or catch a better hand on the turn.

The flop of King-Queen-Eight is highly coordinated and likely hits the opponent's opening range harder than mine. My Ace-Three offsuit has very little showdown value, only Ace-high, and I lack a draw. Checking is the most prudent strategy to maintain pot control, as betting would likely be an unsuccessful bluff against a range that probably contains at least a King, Queen, or a pair of eights. I will check to see the turn card for free.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Tens and Eights). The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (any Jack makes a straight) and a heart flush draw. However, since the flop was checked through, it is unlikely Flashlight has a very strong hand like AK or KQ. I will lead out with a bet of 100 (half the pot) to get value from weaker hands like a single Queen, a Ten, or drawing hands, and to protect my hand against various river cards.

The turn card (Ten of Spades) has opened up a gutshot straight draw, as any Jack will now complete a Broadway straight. With the pot at 300 and a bet of 100 to me, I am receiving 4:1 pot odds, which is a mathematically sound price to call and see the river. My Ace-high provides limited showdown value, but the implied odds of hitting the straight and the direct pot odds justify continuing in the hand.

On the river, the 2c is a total brick that doesn't complete any draws. My two pair (Tens and Eights) is a relatively strong hand in a three-handed game, especially given the passive action on the flop. I bet the turn and was called, which suggests the opponent has a piece of the board (like a King or Queen) or a draw. Since the flop was checked through, it's less likely they have a monster like KQ or a set. I will place a value bet of 200 (half the pot) to get paid by weaker hands like Kx or Qx, while being prepared to fold if faced with a significant raise that suggests a straight.

On the river with Ace-high on a board of King-Queen-Eight-Ten-Deuce, my hand has no showdown value against any pair or better. The opponent's line of checking the flop and betting the turn and river suggests they may have turned a pair or are betting for value with a King or Queen. Since I cannot beat any pair, calling is essentially 'hope-based' and mathematically incorrect given the range of hands the opponent would play this way. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Q4o. In a 3-handed game, the button is a strong position, but Q4o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop and has poor equity against a typical defending range from the blinds. While stealing is an option, I prefer to wait for better starting hands early in the tournament rather than committing chips with a marginal hand like this.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. With King-Three offsuit, completing the small blind is a standard way to see a flop cheaply without inflating the pot with a marginal hand, giving me the opportunity to play post-flop with position in the betting round.

With pocket Queens in the big blind facing a small blind limp, we have a premium starting hand. We should raise to build the pot for value, gain initiative, and prevent the small blind from seeing a cheap flop. A raise to 3-4x the big blind is standard in this spot.

I am in the small blind with King-Three offsuit, which is a weak holding, especially when facing a raise from the big blind. Calling a raise out of position with such a dominated hand is mathematically unfavorable and leads to difficult post-flop decisions. It is better to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Holding Jack-Six offsuit on the button is a weak hand that does not warrant an investment, especially in the early stages of a long tournament. It is mathematically optimal to fold and wait for a more playable hand to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5d offsuit. The Button folded, leaving me to act against the Big Blind. 75o is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Playing this from out of position would be highly unprofitable. The standard and optimal play is to fold.
Jack-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, well below a standard opening range even from the Button in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the Button (Pro) has folded, K7o is a relatively strong hand. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, or to play a pot with a range advantage if Flashlight calls from the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with Ace-six offsuit, which is a strong enough holding to defend against a small blind raise. Folding would be too conservative given the pot odds and my position. Three-betting with this hand out of position against a potentially wide range from the small blind is unnecessary and risky. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity, keeping the pot manageable.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind battle. The board is [Qh 2h 6d], which is relatively good for my perceived range. I have King-high, which still has some showdown value but can't comfortably check-call. I will fire a continuation bet of 200 (1/3 pot) to take down the pot now or to deny equity to Flashlight's random hands that might have missed the flop.

I have a pair of sixes on a board of Qh-2h-6d. Flash's bet of 200 into a 600 pot is a small continuation bet, suggesting they may be betting with a marginal hand or a draw. While my Ace kicker is weak, the pair of sixes is a solid holding against a wide small blind range. Raising here would likely fold out hands I currently beat and isolate me against better hands like a Queen. Calling is the best way to control the pot, see a turn card, and re-evaluate my standing.

The Jack of diamonds is a card that hits some of Flashlight's calling range from the flop (like AJ or J10) and doesn't improve my King-high. Since my flop continuation bet was called, Flashlight likely has a piece of this board or a draw. Continuing with a second barrel is risky here as I have no equity and no draw. I will check to see how Flashlight responds; if they bet, I can comfortably fold, and if they check back, I might still have some showdown value or an opportunity to bluff on the river depending on the card.

The opponent has checked the turn after continuation betting on the flop. Given my hand is only a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker, I do not have enough strength to value bet here. Checking back allows me to control the pot, keep the opponent's range wide, and potentially reach a cheap showdown on the river.

I have King-high on a board of Qh-2h-6d-Jd-6c. I was the pre-flop aggressor and fired a continuation bet on the flop, which was called. After the turn went check-check, it's likely my opponent has some marginal showdown value or a missed draw. While King-high occasionally wins here against missed heart or diamond draws, I don't have enough strength to bet for value. Betting as a bluff is also risky because my opponent called the flop and might not fold a pair of Queens, Jacks, or even Deuces to a single river bet after the turn was checked. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, folding if Flashlight bets.

I have improved to three-of-a-kind (sixes) on the river, which is a very strong hand given the board texture. Flash checked the river after checking the turn, which indicates they likely have a marginal hand (like a Queen or Jack) or are playing a missed draw passively. Checking behind would be too passive; I should bet for value to extract chips from a Queen or a similar middle-pair holding that might pay off a modest bet.

I have King-high on a board of Qh-2h-6d-Jd-6c. After I checked the river, Flashlight bet 500 into a 1000 pot. My King-high is extremely weak here; it loses to any pair and even to Ace-high bluffs. While Flashlight might be bluffing with missed draws, calling with King-high is mathematically and strategically unsound given that I don't beat most of their bluffing range (which often includes Ace-high). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. While button opening ranges are wide in three-handed play, J2o is at the very bottom of the range and generally considered a fold. The risk of being called or re-raised by the blinds outweighs the potential of stealing the blinds with such a weak hand.

I am in the small blind with 7d-3c, which is a very weak hand with almost no equity or playability. Given that I have already posted 50 chips and the big blind is 100, calling would require an additional 50 chips to see a flop against the big blind, which is not a profitable long-term strategy with such a poor holding. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

K5 offsuit is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, K5o lacks the playability and equity required to justify an open-raise or a call against the blinds, who are likely to have broader calling ranges. It is better to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

With Jack-Seven offsuit in the small blind and the button folded, I have a weak hand and will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand if I choose to enter the pot. Folding is the most standard and solid play here to conserve chips rather than completing or raising with a marginal holding.
5d 8s is an uncoordinated, low-value offsuit hand. As the first to act pre-flop on the button, there is no justification for entering the pot with this holding. Folding is the optimal decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although the action has folded to me and I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, 42o realizes equity very poorly out of position. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand than to play a weak range from the small blind against Flashlight.
Holding a pocket pair (55) on the button in a 3-handed game is a strong opening candidate. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative and exert pressure on the blinds.

7-10 offsuit is a weak, non-connected hand with very poor playability, especially since I am in the Small Blind and will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against the Button. Calling a raise of 300 when I only have 50 invested is a negative expected value play. Folding is the disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button, Q9s is a standard defend from the Big Blind. It has good playability post-flop and enough equity against a wide Button opening range. I will call to see a flop heads-up out of position.

I completely missed this paired 7-7-3 flop with Qh 9h. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I have no strong reason to lead out with air, so checking to see what Flash does is the optimal move.

I have a medium pocket pair (55) on a paired board (7s 3c 7c). This is a strong situation to continuation bet. Pro checked, which is expected. By betting around 40% of the pot, I can protect my hand against overcards, extract value from smaller pairs or draws, and potentially take the pot down right now. If Pro has a 7, I will find out soon enough, but my range advantage as the pre-flop raiser justifies the aggression here.

I completely missed the flop, holding just Queen-high with no straight or flush draws. The board is paired and flush-heavy in clubs, while my cards are both hearts, leaving me with zero backdoor equity. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor out of position, the only sensible and standard play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand on the button. I want to build a pot and apply pressure on the blinds. A standard raise to 300 chips is the optimal move to narrow the field and potentially pick up the blinds pre-flop or set up a strong post-flop scenario.
Holding 7s 3h, an extremely weak and uncoordinated offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind. This is a clear fold to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 5s. Flashlight raised to 3x from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 65 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a Button opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament and the depth of stacks, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand that will be difficult to play post-flop.

Queen-Six suited is a very marginal hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. While we would have position post-flop if called, the hand plays poorly against most calling or 3-betting ranges from the blinds. Playing conservatively and folding is the best approach to preserve our stack for stronger hands.

I have a pocket pair of threes in the Small Blind. Since the Button folded, it is a blind-versus-blind situation. A pocket pair is a strong starting hand in this scenario, so I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately.

A7o is a reasonable hand to defend from the big blind against a small blind raise. The pot odds are 2:1, which makes calling mathematically sound. Given the opponent's aggressive history, flatting to see a flop and maintaining control is the preferred approach rather than over-committing with a 3-bet, which might isolate me against a better range.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind situation. The flop of A-K-K is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's perceived range, as I can easily have an Ace or a King. Although my pocket threes are now quite weak, I will continue with a small continuation bet of 200 (1/3 of the pot) to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately if they missed the board. If I am called or raised, I will likely give up the hand.

The opponent's small bet of 200 into an 800 pot provides excellent pot odds of 4:1. With top pair, folding would be overly tight, and raising is unlikely to generate value or fold out better hands. Calling is the standard, disciplined play, allowing me to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the opponent's subsequent action.

The turn card is the Four of Clubs, which completes a possible flush. My pocket threes are extremely weak on this Ace-King-King-Four board. Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which indicates they likely have an Ace, a King, or a flush draw. Given the strength of the board and my opponent's call, another bet is unlikely to succeed as a bluff. I will check and plan to fold if faced with a bet.

The turn has paired the board with a King, making my top pair of Aces even more vulnerable, as any King or better-kicked Ace is beating me. Flash checked, signaling weakness or a desire to control the pot. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (trips or better kickers), while checking allows me to realize my equity and reach the river cheaply. A check is the most disciplined and effective play to manage the pot size.

The board has double-paired with Aces and Kings (Ac-As-Kc-Ks-4c). My pocket threes are now lower than the 4 on the board, meaning my best possible hand is the board itself. Any opponent holding a card higher than a 4 (5 through Queen) will have a better five-card hand than me. Since I checked the turn, it is unlikely I can represent a strong Ace or King convincingly with a bet. I will check and hope Flashlight also has very low cards for a potential chop, but I expect to lose to any high-card hand if they check back, and I will fold to any bet.

I hold Aces full of Kings on a board of A-K-K-4-A. This is an extremely strong hand. My opponent checked the river, which allows me to take the initiative and bet for value. I will bet 500 into the 1000 pot to extract chips from any flush or weaker Ace that might call.

The board has double-paired with Aces and Kings (Ac-As-Kc-Ks-4c). My pocket threes are lower than the four on the board, so my best possible five-card hand is the board itself: two pair, Aces and Kings, with a Four kicker. Flashlight's bet of 500 into the 1000 pot suggests they have a hand that beats the board's kicker. Since almost any hand Flashlight would reasonably have (any card 5 or higher) beats my hand, and it is highly unlikely they are bluffing with a hand weaker than a 4 kicker in this spot, I must fold.

I am on the button with 9d 2d, a weak suited hand. While it has some potential in a multi-way pot due to its suit, it is generally too weak to open or call a raise with in most scenarios. However, the action is currently on me and I am the dealer. Since I have 95 big blinds, I could consider a steal, but 92s is near the bottom of my range. Looking at previous hands, the blinds have shown some aggression. I will fold this hand to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with 4h-2c, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even though I have already posted 50 chips to the pot, calling another 50 to play out of position against the big blind with such a poor holding is a negative expectation play. It is better to fold and preserve my stack.
J9o is a playable hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. I will open for 250, which is 2.5x the big blind, to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three offsuit facing a raise from the Button. This hand is extremely weak and has terrible playability out of position. The standard and only profitable play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation against a Button open. The Button has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me great pot odds. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and play a strong hand post-flop against a wide button-opening range.

I am first to act on a flop of As-6s-Th. I hold King-Queen offsuit, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and two overcards to the middle cards, though an Ace is present on the board. Since Flashlight raised from the button pre-flop, their range contains many Aces. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this board texture is rarely optimal. I will check to see how the button reacts, aiming to realize my equity as cheaply as possible or potentially check-call a small bet given the gutshot and backdoor possibilities.

I have a flush draw on an Ace-high board with my Nine of Spades. This is an excellent opportunity for a continuation bet to represent the Ace in my range, which is highly credible given my pre-flop opening, and to gain equity if I hit my flush.

I am facing a standard continuation bet from the button on an Ace-high board. While I only have King-high, I possess a gutshot straight draw to the broadway nuts (any Jack). The bet size of 250 into a pot of 550 gives me reasonable odds to see a turn card, especially considering the implied odds if I hit my straight. My previous plan was to check-call a small bet to realize my equity, and this sizing allows for that. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am out of position on a turn card that does not significantly improve my hand. I still hold King-high with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. Since Flashlight was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, it is highly likely they will continue their aggression if they have an Ace or a draw. Leading into them now would be risky and likely wouldn't fold out better hands. I will check to see their action and decide based on their bet sizing whether to continue chasing my gutshot.

The turn card is the Seven of Clubs, which adds a gutshot straight draw to my flush draw. Flash has checked to me on the turn. With an Ace on the board, my opponent likely has some piece of the board, making a bluff difficult to execute successfully. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and potentially see a free river card to hit my outs.

The river is a 9, making the final board As-6s-Th-7c-9c. I missed my gutshot straight draw and I am left with King-high, which has virtually no showdown value. Flashlight checked back on the turn, which often indicates a marginal made hand (like a Ten, a Seven, or a weak Ace) that is looking to get to showdown cheaply, or a total air hand that has given up. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a straight (holding an 8) or a slow-played strong Ace, bluffing into an Ace-high board is generally risky as opponents find it difficult to fold any pair. Given the tournament context and my current stack, checking is the most disciplined play. If Flashlight checks back, I likely lose to their marginal holdings; if they bet, I will fold as my hand cannot beat any value bet or even most bluffs.

My pair of nines is very weak on this Ace-high board, especially since the opponent called my continuation bet on the flop. Given that the opponent has checked down the turn and river, they likely have a piece of the board that they are playing cautiously or a missed draw. Betting now would only be called by hands that have me beaten, so I will check behind to take the showdown and avoid putting more chips into the pot with a marginal hand.

98o is too weak of a hand to open from the Button even in 3-handed play. The offsuit connectors have poor playability against 3-bets and tend to make dominated pairs or marginal draws. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve the stack.

K6s is a strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. With the button folding, I am facing only the Big Blind. Standard strategy dictates an aggressive open to take down the blinds or gain the initiative for post-flop play. I will raise to 300.

K4 offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an open raise. The potential for being dominated is high, and the pot odds don't justify a call given the weakness of the hand. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 95o, which is a very weak hand. While positional advantage is significant in 3-handed play, 95o does not have enough equity to justify a call or a raise against the blinds, especially after I recently successfully stole the blinds in the previous hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. Holding Ace-Four suited, I have a significant equity advantage and positional initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the hand, potentially forcing a fold from the big blind or allowing me to define the range and size of the pot effectively given my stack depth.

I have Ace-Queen suited in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open. This is a premium hand that is significantly ahead of the Small Blind's opening range. 3-betting for value and to take control of the hand is the best play. Sizing up to 900 puts pressure on marginal hands and builds the pot with a strong holding in position.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind while in the small blind with A4s is a tricky spot. While A4s is dominated by higher aces and pairs, it retains good equity against a wide range and has strong potential for post-flop playability. Given the pot odds (calling 600 to win a pot that will be 1,800), calling is a mathematically sound decision to see a flop, rather than risking a 4-bet bluff out of position or folding prematurely.

The flop is somewhat dry but favors the 3-bettor's range, which likely contains high cards and pocket pairs that connect with this board. Holding only Ace-high out of position, leading into the aggressor is non-optimal as I would be inflating the pot with a weak hand that will struggle to realize equity if called. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's continuation strategy.

I missed the flop but hold two strong overcards with backdoor diamond equity. The board of 3h Js 6s is somewhat coordinated, and without any spades in my hand, I don't block the flush draws Flashlight could hold. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity by seeing a turn card for free, and avoids being check-raised off a hand that still has decent potential to improve.

The opponent (Pro) 3-bet pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting either a slow play with a strong hand (like QQ+) or that they missed with high cards like AK/AQ. With Ace-high, I have some showdown value but not enough to bet for value against a calling range, and betting into a potential trap is risky. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot controlled, realize my equity, and potentially take the pot at showdown if the river is safe.

With Ace-Queen high and no draw other than overcards, checking again is the most prudent play. Flashlight checking twice indicates he likely has a marginal made hand (like a medium pocket pair or a weak Jack) or a drawing hand like a missed flush draw. By checking, I keep the pot small, get a free card to try and hit my Ace or Queen on the river, and maintain my showdown value against missed draws. A bet here might only get called by better hands or strong draws, and risks being check-raised out of the pot.

With both players having checked the flop and turn, the board has reached the river without much action. My Ace-high, pairing the Jack on the board, has some marginal showdown value, but is unlikely to be the best hand if the opponent has any pair. Betting here would effectively turn my hand into a bluff, which is ill-advised given the lack of information on the opponent's strength. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize losses against a potentially stronger hand or capture the pot against total air.

With Ace-Queen high on a paired board where multiple draws missed (such as spade flush draws and straight draws like T9), I have solid showdown value. Flashlight checking three streets suggests a capped range, likely containing medium pocket pairs, weak pairs, or busted draws. A bluff here might get called by stubborn pairs like 99 or TT, and I already beat the missed draws. Checking behind ensures I get to showdown without risking more chips, capitalizing on the hands I already beat.

King-three suited is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing two opponents. It lacks sufficient high-card strength and connectedness to justify investing chips from the button. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

Holding 97o in the small blind after the button folds is a weak starting hand. Being out of position against the Big Blind with an unsuited and disconnected hand makes playing it unprofitable. The standard and safest action here is to fold.
J9o is a weak, unsuited hand that doesn't play very well post-flop. Being first to act pre-flop, the standard and safest move to conserve my stack is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o). Since the Button has folded, the action is on me. A5o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to put pressure on Flashlight and either take down the blinds now or play the pot with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind and facing a raise from the Small Blind. Holding Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to defend against a Small Blind open. The pot odds are favorable to call (200 to win a total of 400), and A8o plays well enough to see a flop and re-evaluate.

On this paired board of Kc-5h-Ks, I have two pair (Kings and Fives) with an Ace kicker. While the Kings are dangerous if my opponent has one, the board is dry. I will make a standard continuation bet of 200 (1/3 of the pot) to gain information and extract value from hands like pocket pairs lower than Kings or Ace-high hands that might call. If I encounter significant resistance, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

The board is K-K-5, which is quite dry and favors the Small Blind's opening range. However, my opponent is only betting 200 into a 600 pot. This small sizing suggests a continuation bet that could be a King, a pocket pair, or a complete bluff. With Ace-high, I have decent showdown value and folding to such a small bet would be overly conservative. Calling allows me to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn, while avoiding folding to a potentially weak hand or a bluff.

On the turn, the board now shows three Kings (Kc, Ks, Kd) and a Five (5h). Holding Ah and 5d, I have a full house: Kings full of Fives. This is an extremely strong hand, though it is vulnerable to any pocket pair higher than 55 (which would make a higher full house) or the case King (which would make quads). Given the action on the flop where I bet and Flashlight called, it's unlikely they are sitting on the last King, as they likely would have raised or slow-played differently. I want to extract value from Ace-high hands or smaller pocket pairs that might be calling. Checking could induce a bluff, but betting allows me to dictate the price and build the pot. I will check to see if Flashlight bets, planning to call or raise depending on their sizing and the board development, as a check-call line can often disguise the strength of a full house on such a board.

The board shows three Kings (Kc, Ks, Kd) and a 5h. Flash checked the turn. Given that Flash was the pre-flop aggressor, they very likely have a King in their range, which would make them effectively unbeatable with my Ace-high. Even if they don't have a King, betting into them is unlikely to generate value, as I will only be called by better hands. Checking behind is the optimal play to take a free river card and manage the pot size.

The board has tripled the Kings (Kc-Ks-Kd) and also contains a Five (5h) and a Three (3d). With my Ah-5d, my best 5-card hand is Kings full of Fives (Kd-Ks-Kc-5h-5d). This is a very strong hand, only losing to a pocket pair higher than 5-5 (making a higher full house) or the case King (making quad Kings). Given the action (check-check on the turn), it is unlikely Flashlight has a King. Flashlight has shown a tendency in previous hands (Hand #4 and Hand #10) to bet aggressively on the river when checked to. By checking, I am attempting to induce a bluff from Ace-high hands or a value bet from lower pairs that might think they are ahead or trying to represent the King. If Flashlight checks back, I still win against most of their range. If they bet, I have a strong enough hand to call or potentially raise.

The board shows three Kings (Kc, Ks, Kd) and low cards (5h, 3d), meaning it is highly connected to any hand containing a King. With my Ace-high, I am tied with any other player who does not hold a King, as we are both playing the board. If I bet, I will only be called by players holding the final King (making four of a kind) or another Ace, leading to a split pot. Since Flash has checked the turn and the river, it is highly likely that this is a split pot scenario or that they are trap-checking a King. Checking behind is the most conservative and optimal play to avoid unnecessary losses and ensure a split pot if both players hold the same strength.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While opening from the button is generally encouraged to steal the blinds, 9-2 is far too weak to play profitably even in a 3-handed game. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With a weak hand like Jack-six offsuit and acting out of position from the small blind against the big blind, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision. I am in position with a pair of Tens and a flush draw on a board of 3s 3c Ks Th. The opponent, who was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, has checked the turn. This check likely indicates either a lack of a strong King, a desire to trap, or a defensive stance. Given the strength of my draw and the marginal value of my pair, checking behind is the most prudent decision. It allows me to realize my equity for free, control the size of the pot, and avoid bloating the pot against a potential trapped hand or stronger King, while still maintaining the opportunity to hit my flush on the river.

The river card is a blank. My Ace-Jack high has marginal showdown value against missed draws like flush draws or straight draws. Betting here would essentially turn my hand into a bluff, and it's unlikely to fold out medium pocket pairs or a Ten that checked back the turn for pot control. Checking allows me to realize whatever showdown equity I have against his missed draws, and I can easily fold if he makes a substantial bet.

On the river, the board is 3s 3c Ks Th 6d. I hold 8s Ts, giving me a pair of tens. My opponent (Pro) has checked the river after checking the turn, indicating potential weakness or a desire to induce a bluff. With a marginal made hand like a pair of tens, betting is unlikely to yield value from worse hands, and it risks being check-raised by a better hand. Therefore, checking back to showdown is the optimal play to minimize losses and capitalize on the times my pair of tens is ahead.
JTo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Button in 3-handed play. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) applies pressure to the blinds and gives us the initiative heading into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 5s, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Folding this hand in this position is the standard play as it has very poor equity and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if I were to call.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit against a button open from Pro. The pot odds are favorable to call (200 to call into a total pot of 450). A9o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind, as it has high-card strength and potential to hit top pair. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind and the flop is Q-T-6 rainbow. I have Ace-high with no pair or strong draw. It is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor in this position to control the pot size and re-evaluate on later streets.

With middle pair on a somewhat coordinated board (Q-T-6), checking back is a solid pot-control line. It allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, realize my equity, and avoid getting check-raised by stronger hands like Qx or better Tx. I can evaluate the turn to see if I improve or face a bet.

I have improved to a pair of nines on the turn, but this is a weak holding on a board that contains several connected possibilities (like 8-7, J-8, or K-J). Since the pot is small and my opponent checked the flop, I prefer checking to control the pot size and reach showdown cost-effectively. Betting here likely forces out weaker hands that I currently beat and gets called by better hands, so checking is the most balanced strategic approach.

After checking the flop for pot control, the turn 9d gives us an open-ended straight draw to go with our middle pair. Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting a capped or marginal range. Betting here serves as a strong semi-bluff and value bet combo: it denies equity to overcards, charges straight draws or weaker pairs, and builds the pot in case we hit our straight on the river.

The opponent's bet of 300 into a 950 pot is relatively small, providing favorable pot odds to continue. While a pair of nines is a marginal holding on this board, the opponent's check on the flop suggests a lack of a strong made hand, potentially indicating a semi-bluff or a weak ten. Calling is a balanced, cost-effective approach to see the river and evaluate the final board state, avoiding inflating the pot unnecessarily with a vulnerable pair.

With a pair of nines on a highly connected board (K-Q-T-9-6), my hand has marginal showdown value. There are several straight possibilities and overpairs that beat my nines. As the out-of-position player who called the turn bet, I should check to the aggressor. Checking allows me to control the pot size and keeps the range of hands I face wide, as I cannot profitably bet for value and would essentially be turning my hand into a bluff if I were to bet.

On the river, the King of Clubs completes my straight (9-T-J-Q-K). The only hand that beats me is A-J, which is relatively unlikely given the preflop action and passive play throughout the hand. Flashlight's turn call and river check suggest he might have a pair of Kings, Queens, two pair, or a weaker straight like J-8. A value bet of roughly two-thirds of the pot is perfectly sized to extract maximum value from these marginal to strong hands.

The board has become extremely connected with the arrival of the King on the river, completing potential straight draws (such as J-x combinations) and significantly strengthening the opponent's range. My pair of nines is effectively a weak bluff-catcher that loses to any King, Queen, or Ten, all of which are very likely in the opponent's button opening range. Given that the opponent demonstrated strength by betting on both the turn and the river, it is highly probable that my hand is beaten, and calling here would be a negative expected value play.

Holding 9d 5c on the button, this hand is significantly below the opening range for a three-handed game. While the button is the best position to steal blinds, 9-5 offsuit lacks the equity to be a profitable raise against observant opponents like Flashlight and Pro. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with As7s. This is a strong hand that plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Calling the extra 50 chips to see a flop is a standard, low-risk way to play this hand given my solid chip lead and position.

I have pocket Aces in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp. This is a premium hand, so I will raise to build the pot and extract value. A raise to 3.5x is standard to punish the limp and define the range.

I am in the small blind with a suited Ace (As7s). Given the opponent's aggressive pre-flop raises in previous hands, calling the 350 total (matching the big blind's raise) is the most prudent move. It keeps the pot manageable, allows me to see a flop, and avoids unnecessary risk while playing out of position against an active opponent.

I am out of position against 'Pro' on a paired board of 9s-9h-7d. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Since I am first to act on this flop, checking is the standard play to gauge Pro's reaction to the board. If Pro bets, I can re-evaluate my pair of sevens, but betting into the aggressor here risks bloating a pot with a marginal hand.

The board is paired and relatively dry, making it unlikely for my opponent to have hit it hard unless they specifically hold a 9. I have a strong overpair with pocket Aces. A smaller c-bet sizing around 1/3 to 40% of the pot (250 chips) is optimal here to extract value from smaller pocket pairs (like 88 or TT) and straight draws (like T8 or 86) while controlling the size of the pot in case I am up against trips.

The board texture is 9s-9h-7d, which is quite dry and favors the pre-flop aggressor. I have a pair of sevens with an Ace kicker. While this is not a powerhouse hand, the pot odds (calling 250 into a 950 pot) are favorable, and I am in position relative to the betting action. Pro could easily be c-betting with overcards or air here. Folding would be too conservative given the small bet size, and raising would likely only isolate me against a range that crushes my pair. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops.

The board texture is 9-9-7-8, which is highly coordinated and dangerous. With a pair of sevens (A-7 kicker), I have some showdown value but am vulnerable to many hands that Pro would have in their range, such as any nine, an eight, or straight draws like 6-5 or 10-6. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see if Pro checks behind or bets. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or induce a raise that I cannot call.

The turn 8d makes the board highly coordinated (9s-9h-7d-8d), hitting my opponent's limp-calling range perfectly. Any Ten or Six makes a straight, and there are many 9x combinations, straight draws, and flush draws in their range. Betting with pocket Aces here risks getting check-raised by better hands, and few worse hands will call a value bet. Checking behind for pot control is the best option to realize equity and keep the pot small when the board is this dangerous.

The river Ace of Diamonds is a great card for my hand, giving me top two pair (Aces and Nines). The turn was checked through, which suggests neither player has a monster hand like a full house (which would likely have bet). Given the board is not overly coordinated and my two pair is strong, I will place a value bet of 600 chips to extract value from any worse Ace or a pair of eights that might call.

I rivered the top full house (Aces full of Nines) when the Ace of Diamonds hit, making my Ah Ac nearly unbeatable. The only hand that beats me is exactly quad Nines (99). My opponent's lead of 600 into a 1200 pot suggests they have a strong hand they are betting for value, such as a rivered flush, a straight, trips (9x), or a smaller full house. Since I have the effective nuts, raising is mandatory to extract maximum value. A raise to 2400 gives them a reasonable price to call with their strong second-best hands without scaring them off like an all-in shove might.

I have top two pair (Aces and Nines) on a board of 9-9-7-8-A. Pro's check on the turn makes it unlikely they have a monster hand like a full house or trip nines, as a strong player would typically bet for value on the turn or with a draw. By raising the river, Pro could be overplaying a weaker Ace (e.g., A-K, A-Q, A-J) or potentially turning a missed draw into a bluff. Given the pot odds and the likelihood that my hand is often good here, calling is the most profitable play.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to open the action. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds, leveraging my position.

With Ten-Seven suited in the small blind facing a 3x open from the button, this hand is too weak to profitably call or 3-bet. Playing a suited one-gapper out of position is a losing proposition, especially with the big blind still to act. The most prudent action is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Nine-Ten offsuit. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. These are very good pot odds (better than 2-to-1), and 9-10 offsuit is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a Button open in a three-handed game. I will call to see a flop.

On a board of 6s-9d-6h, I have top pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against a Button raiser. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to allow them to continue with their bluffs or bet with weaker hands. If Flashlight bets, I will comfortably call to see the turn, as my hand is too strong to fold but raising might drive away their bluffs.

I am on the button with Ace-high on a paired board (6s 9d 6h). My opponent has checked to me. A bet here would likely only be called by a 6, a 9, or a better pocket pair, none of which I beat. Betting as a pure bluff is risky given the paired board. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, potentially catching an Ace or an 8 on the turn or river to improve to the best hand, or checking down to showdown if the board runs out safely.

I have top pair (Nines) on a paired board. Flashlight checked back the flop after raising pre-flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value but not enough to bet (like a lower pair or Ace-high) or a total miss. Since I likely have the best hand, I will lead out on the turn for value. A bet of roughly half the pot should be called by any pair or high-card hands that Flashlight is 'pot-controlling' with, while also protecting against straight draws like 7-8.

The board is paired with sixes, and my Ace-high has completely missed. Flash checked the flop, which often suggests either a weak hand or a trap, and leading the turn into me suggests they have paired the nine, the six, or are playing a small pocket pair aggressively. With no pair and only an overcard that may not be clean if my opponent has a nine, calling a bet of 35% of the pot is a losing play. I prefer to fold here and preserve my stack for a better spot.
QTo is a reasonable starting hand from the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll open with a standard raise to 300 to steal the blinds or take control of the hand post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Ten offsuit (KTo). This is a strong enough hand to continue against a Button raise in a 3-handed game. While a 3-bet is an option to take the lead, calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable while playing out of position. KTo has decent high-card value and flopability in this spot. I will call the raise and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand. Calling out of position with such a dominated hand would lead to difficult post-flop decisions and poor equity realization. A fold is the most disciplined and standard play here.

On this paired board of 6d-6c-7d, I have two overcards (King and Ten) but have not directly connected. On a paired 6-6-7 board, my QTo hasn't connected directly, but I still have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Pro's continuation bet of 250 into 700 is relatively small, approximately 35% of the pot. On a paired board like 6d-6c-7d, the pre-flop aggressor is expected to bet frequently. Folding at this point would be too conservative given the pot odds and the likelihood that Pro is betting with a wide range that I still have equity against. I will call the bet to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the next street's action.

I have King-high on a board that hasn't improved my hand. The turn Jack of Spades doesn't help me, and I'm playing out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Standard play here is to check and see how the opponent reacts. I don't want to build a pot with a marginal hand that currently has no pair or significant draw.

The turn brings the Jack of spades, which is a good card for my perceived pre-flop raising range, but I only have Queen-Ten high. While a double-barrel bluff might fold out some floats or smaller pairs, it risks getting called or check-raised by a hand that has me crushed. On the river with the board 6d-6c-7d-Js-8c, I hold King-Ten offsuit. The board is paired (sixes) and includes several straight possibilities. With Queen-Ten high on a 6-6-7-J-8 board, I have essentially no showdown value, losing to any pair and A-high. I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position against one of the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with K-2 offsuit against a button open-raise from Flash. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button. My hand is 4d 3s, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Calling or raising here would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the only correct play.

King-Queen offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, particularly from the button position. It is well within my opening range, and I want to take the initiative by raising to 300 to steal the blinds and build a pot.

I am dealt Ten-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I have no equity to justify a call or a 3-bet, especially out of position. Folding is the only optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5h 6c. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. To call, I need to put in 200 more. Given the tournament context and my current stack, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending such a marginal hand.
With A6o on the button in 3-handed play, I have a clear open-raise to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position with an Ace-high hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Pro has raised to 300 from the Button. J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a standard button opening range. Seven-ten offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the big blind against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a dominated hand range. Folding is the optimal strategic decision here.

I am on the button with 53 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. Given the current stack depths and the aggressive play of my opponents, folding is the most prudent and standard professional action to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Jack-three offsuit) and the button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. While the pot odds are tempting, playing a dominated hand out of position against a competent opponent is suboptimal. I will fold to preserve my stack.

My starting hand, Queen-Three offsuit, is weak and not suitable for open-raising or calling in a three-handed game, even from the button. With 85 big blinds remaining, I can afford to be patient and wait for a higher-equity starting hand before committing chips to the pot, rather than engaging with a marginal hand.

65s is a strong suited connector to play aggressively in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind, attempting to steal the pot directly while building a pot in case I get called, as this hand has excellent post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 8h. The Button folded, and the Small Blind (Pro) raised to 3x (300). A continuation bet will maintain the initiative, apply pressure to any marginal holdings in my opponent's range, and build the pot in case my draw completes. A half-pot sizing of 300 works well to balance my bluffs and value bets.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high (Qh 8h) on a board of Jack of Clubs, Ace of Spades, Nine of Spades. The Small Blind (Pro) has led out with a 1/3 pot continuation bet (300 into 900). This flop favors his range as the pre-flop raiser, especially with an Ace and a Jack present. I have a gutshot straight draw (any Ten gives me a straight), but Queen-high with no flush draw on this board is quite weak. Given that Pro has been aggressive and the board is very coordinated, a call here with just a gutshot and an overcard (if he has a Jack or lower) is marginal. However, folding to a single small bet after defending the big blind is often too tight. That said, I have no piece of this board, and if I call, I will likely face another bet on the turn. Without a pair or a flush draw to go with my gutshot, and facing an opponent who can easily have an Ace or a Jack, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
K2 offsuit is a very weak hand, even in 3-handed play. From the button, it is best to simply fold and wait for better starting hands to steal or play for a pot.

The button folded, leaving me in a blind-versus-blind situation. Queen-Jack offsuit (QJo) is a strong hand in this context, significantly ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or build a pot where I have a range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the pot odds to call, 4-5 offsuit does not play well post-flop, and folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here against the Small Blind's raise.

I am on the button with QJ offsuit, which is a very strong opening hand in 3-handed play. Standard play is to raise to 3 times the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. My stack is healthy, and this is a standard value-opening range.

Holding J2o in the small blind is a clear fold against a button raise. The hand has poor connectivity, low equity, and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling here would be a negative expected value decision, and I have no reason to 3-bet with this range.

Flash is opening from the Button very frequently with a standard 3x size. Against such a wide opening range, A9o has strong raw equity. Sizing to 900 (3x their open) gives them a bad price to continue with marginal hands.

I opened to 300 with QJo from the button, and Pro 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind. While QJo is often a marginal hand against 3-bets, previous hands (specifically Hand #14) suggest Pro is capable of 3-betting with a very wide range, including hands as weak as 95o. Given the pot odds (calling 600 to win a total pot of 1250) and the fact that I have position for the rest of the hand, I have enough equity and playability to see a flop. Folding would be too tight against a player who has shown aggressive tendencies with weak holdings.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a strong range advantage on this Q-high board. While my specific hand (A9o) missed completely and lacks backdoor equity, a small continuation bet of around 1/3 pot applies pressure on Flash's floated overcards and underpairs, often taking down the pot right here. If he calls or raises, I can easily give up.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board (Q-6-2). After Pro's 3-bet pre-flop, a small continuation bet of 600 into 1850 (about 1/3 pot) is standard. Given Hand #14, where Pro 3-bet with a hand as weak as 9-5 offsuit, I must assume their range is very wide. I have no reason to fold top pair here. Raising might isolate me against better hands like AQ, KK, or AA, whereas calling allows Pro to continue bluffing with air or worse hands on the turn. I will call to keep the pot manageable and maintain position.

The King on the turn hits a significant portion of Flash's flop floating range, such as AK, KQ, and KJ. While it is a good barrel card if I had equity or a stronger range advantage, I currently have Ace-high with no draws. Attempting to bluff off his medium pairs or weak Qx will be costly and often unsuccessful, especially since he might have picked up top pair. Checking to surrender the hand is the most prudent play.

The King on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Queens and is a card that hits a standard 3-betting range very hard. After Pro 3-bet pre-flop and continued on the flop, their check on the turn King is interesting. While Hand #14 showed Pro is capable of 3-betting with very weak hands like 9-5 offsuit, the King is still a dangerous card. By checking back, I exercise pot control with my second pair, avoid a potential check-raise, and keep the pot manageable while heading to the river. This allows me to see a showdown or evaluate a river bet more effectively without over-committing to a pot where I may now be behind.

On the river, I hit a pair of nines, giving me third pair on this board. Since the turn checked through, Flash's range is heavily weighted towards marginal showdown value hands like pocket tens or jacks, weak queens, or busted draws (e.g., missed diamonds or straight draws like JTs). Value betting here is far too thin as no worse hand will realistically call a bet. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply and potentially catch a bluff from his missed draws.

I have second pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 2-Q-6-K-9. After the 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, Pro has checked both the turn and the river. While Pro has shown a wide 3-betting range in previous hands (like Hand #14 with 9-5o), the King on the turn is a scare card that hits a standard 3-betting range well. By checking the river, Pro is likely either giving up on a bluff or looking to check-call with a medium-strength hand. My hand has significant showdown value, but if I bet, I am unlikely to be called by anything worse than a Queen with a lower kicker, and I might get called by better hands like AQ, KQ, or even a slow-played King. Checking back ensures I reach showdown and win the pot against bluffs and marginal hands without risking a check-raise or value-owning myself.

King-Jack offsuit is a strong holding on the button in a 3-handed game. Opening with a standard 3x raise to 300 is the best strategic play to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds or build a pot in position.

Facing a 3x raise from the button, K6s is too weak to call from the small blind as we'll be out of position and vulnerable to a squeeze from the big blind. It's also slightly too weak to 3-bet bluff consistently. Folding is the most straightforward and disciplined decision.

I have a pocket pair of deuces in the big blind. Flashlight raised from the button, which often represents a wide range in 3-handed play. I am getting roughly 3.25:1 pot odds to call. Pocket deuces are a classic set-mining hand, and given the stack depths and the price, it is profitable to see a flop. I will call the additional 200 to see if I can hit a set or navigate a low board.

I have a pocket pair of deuces on an 8-T-4 rainbow board. This board is relatively dry but contains two overcards to my pair. Flashlight, who raised pre-flop, will have many hands in their range that have missed this board, but also several that have me crushed (higher pocket pairs, or any 8, T, or 4). If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate the bet size, but for now, checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a free turn card if they check back.

The board of 8-10-4 rainbow is relatively dry and does not strongly favor the big blind's calling range. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage. Checking back is an option, but a standard continuation bet of roughly half the pot (300) is the most consistent play to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot immediately, as my opponent checked to me.

I have pocket deuces on a flop of T-8-4, which contains three overcards to my pair. I only have two outs to improve to a set, and almost any turn card will be an overcard to my pair, making it hard to reach showdown profitably. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand (9c 6s). With no prior action, 96o is significantly below the bottom of any standard opening range. The best play is to fold and save my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation after the Button has folded. Raising is the standard play here to exert pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with my range advantage. I will raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) to take control of the hand.

My hand, Queen of Spades and Trey of Clubs, is very weak and lacks connectivity or suit potential. Defending the big blind with such a poor holding against an aggressive opponent's raise is negative expected value. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with a suited King (Ks 2s). In 3-handed play, suited Kings are strong enough to open for a raise to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and appropriate for my stack size.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, even out of position in the small blind.
