With an Ace and a suited kicker, I have several options: I can call to keep the pot smaller and play post-flop, or I can 3-bet to take control. Given my large stack size (15,450) and the relatively shallow stack of Pro (9,300), calling is a solid play that allows me to realize equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a strong opponent, but a 3-bet is also very viable here to put pressure on. However, I'll choose to call here to disguise the strength of my hand and play a pot where I have decent equity and post-flop playability.

I am in the big blind with 9-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call a 700 pot), playing this hand out of position in a three-way pot is extremely negative expectation. It is best to fold and preserve my remaining stack for better opportunities.

On this flop of 6c-Jd-Jh, I have flopped bottom pair with an Ace kicker. While I have improved, the board is paired with Jacks, which is a significant part of a Button opening range. Standard play here as the out-of-position caller is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and see how they react. Leading out (donk betting) here would often be met with raises or calls from better hands and doesn't accomplish much in terms of information or protection. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet, given my pair and the back-door potential of my Ace.

The board is paired with Jacks and very dry. As the pre-flop raiser, I have a significant range advantage here. A small continuation bet will likely fold out a lot of Flash's unpaired hands and random floating hands that missed the flop completely. Betting around 250 chips into the 700 chip pot applies enough pressure for cheap.

On a board of 6c-Jd-Jh, I have bottom pair (Sixes) with an Ace kicker. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 700 (now 950). This is a relatively small sizing (about 1/3 to 1/4 pot), which is common for a range-based C-bet on a paired board. While the Jacks are definitely in the Button's opening range, I cannot fold bottom pair with an Ace kicker to such a small bet. Raising here is risky as it might isolate me against a Jack or an overpair. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, realize my equity, and see how the action develops on the turn. I have sufficient pot odds to continue.

On the turn, the 8h is a somewhat neutral card, though it does complete a potential straight for 97 and introduces a heart flush draw. My hand, bottom pair with an Ace kicker, retains some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop aggressor. Checking remains the most prudent line to control the pot size and allow Pro to continue bluffing with air or check back with weaker marginal hands. If I bet, I risk being raised by a Jack or an overpair, or forcing out the hands I am currently beating. I will check and evaluate based on Pro's action and sizing.

With King-high and no draws on a board of 6c Jd Jh 8h, my hand has very little equity against a calling range. The opponent called the flop c-bet, which could mean a pocket pair, a six, or a float. The turn 8h introduces flush draws and straight draws, reducing my fold equity. Checking back controls the pot and allows me to see a free river, preserving my stack for better spots.

The river is the 3s, which is a total brick and doesn't change the board texture significantly. On this board of J-J-6-8-3, my pair of sixes holds some showdown value, especially after Pro checked back on the turn. This check-back by the pre-flop aggressor often indicates a marginal hand like a middle pocket pair (77-TT) or an Ace-high hand that has given up. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by hands I beat (like Ace-high) and will only be called or raised by hands that beat me (Jacks or higher pairs). Therefore, checking is the best course of action to reach showdown cheaply or potentially catch a bluff if Pro decides to bet their air.

After checking back the turn, betting the river might look like a bluff and could easily be hero-called by small pocket pairs, 6x, or even Ace-high. King-high still retains some showdown value against missed flush draws or straight draws that Flash might have floated with on the flop. Checking behind controls the pot and ensures we get to showdown without risking more chips on a low-equity bluff.

I am on the button with a decent hand (Js 9h) in a 3-handed game. Opening with a raise is standard here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially steal the pot. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to take these aggressive spots. Flashlight and Pro have both folded to button raises frequently in previous hands.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 10-2 suited. Calling 250 chips to play a weak hand out of position against a button raise with the big blind still to act is a negative expected value (-EV) play. Folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic decision here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. My hand, 2h 6d, is completely unplayable and one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Calling or raising here would be a huge long-term losing play. The standard and optimal choice is an immediate fold.
I am in the button position with 8h 2s, which is an extremely weak hand. There is no strategic value in playing this hand, so folding is the correct and disciplined decision.

Folded to me in the Small Blind with J6 offsuit. This hand is too weak to play, even in a blind versus blind scenario, especially against the big stack (Flash). The mathematically correct and standard play is to fold.
On the button with QTo, a standard open-raise is profitable. It pressures the blinds and gives me the initiative and positional advantage post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The button (Pro) has raised to 300. K7o is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a Button raise, as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. However, given the current stack sizes and the 3-handed nature of the game, K7o has decent equity against a Button opening range. Folding is safe, but calling is acceptable to see a flop. Raising would be a very aggressive play but probably unnecessary here. I will call the additional 200 to see the flop.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand (J6 offsuit). Facing a raise from the button (Pro) and a call from the small blind (Flash), I am out of position against two opponents. With poor pot odds and a dominated hand, folding is the most profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a very coordinated monotone board (three hearts). Since I do not hold a heart in my hand, I have no redraw to a flush and am vulnerable to any opponent holding a single heart or a made flush. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot and see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts to this texture.

I hold the Queen of Hearts on a monotone heart board, giving me the second nut flush draw. This is an excellent spot to make a continuation bet as a semi-bluff. It puts pressure on any of Flash's hands that don't contain a high heart, and I have great equity to improve to a very strong flush if called.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a monotone board (9h-2h-7h). While I do not hold a heart, which limits my potential to improve and makes my hand vulnerable, the bet from the pre-flop aggressor is relatively small (250 into a pot of 700). Folding middle pair to a single small continuation bet would be too tight and exploitable in a 3-handed game. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and the opponent's subsequent action.

On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds is a significant scare card. As the pre-flop aggressor on the button, Pro's range contains many Aces (Ax) that have now improved. I currently hold third pair (sevens) with no heart, meaning I have no redraw to a flush and am extremely vulnerable to any heart in the opponent's hand. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands or forced out by a raise. Checking is the most prudent action to see how the button reacts to the Ace. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but for now, I will check and play defensively.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a great card for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, but it also heavily connects with the hands Flash might have floated the flop with, such as an Ace with a single heart. Since I hold the second nut flush draw (Qh) but no current showdown value, taking a free card here is the safest play. It prevents me from being check-raised off my draw while allowing me to realize my equity and evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board is 9h-2h-7h-Ad-6s. I hold a pair of sevens with a King kicker. The action on the turn was check-check after the Ace of Diamonds appeared, which suggests that Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) likely does not have a strong Ace or is exercising pot control with a marginal hand. By checking the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown with my middle pair. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a nine or a weak Ace) and wouldn't provide much value against hands I already beat. I will check and hope my sevens are good.

I have Queen-high and no showdown value. Flash called my flop continuation bet but has now checked both the turn and the river. His line heavily suggests a marginal made hand, such as a middle pair (9x, 7x, or a pocket pair), or a busted draw. Since I hold the Qh, I block many of the strong flush combinations he could have. By betting the river, I can represent an Ace that pot-controlled on the turn or a delayed value bet, applying significant pressure to his capped range. A bet of around 2/3 pot should be enough to fold out his weak one-pair hands and win the pot without a showdown.

The board texture is highly unfavorable for a pair of sevens with no heart. While the turn checked through, which might suggest Pro is not holding a top-tier hand like a flush or a strong Ace, the river bet of 800 into a 1200 pot is a significant sizing. My hand, third pair with no heart, serves as a poor bluff-catcher because I do not block any of the likely value hands (flushes, nines, or aces). Pro's turn check could easily be pot control with a marginal hand that now beats me, such as a Nine or a weak Ace, or even a medium pocket pair like Tens or Jacks. Given the risk-to-reward ratio and my weak relative hand strength, folding is the most prudent play to maintain my stack lead.

I am on the button with 5c3c. This is a speculative suited connector (gapped), and the stack sizes of my opponents are moderate to low, especially Flashlight who is at 4600. While I could open-raise, my recent history shows I have been quite active. Given the 50/100 structure and my hand's weak high-card value, I will fold to maintain my lead and wait for a better spot to put pressure on the shorter stacks.

With King-Jack offsuit in the small blind, I am heads-up against the big blind. This is a strong starting hand in this position, and I want to take the initiative pre-flop to either steal the blinds or take control of the hand. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind.

With 9h5h, my hand is too weak and disconnected to call a 3x raise even from the Small Blind. I am out of position to the Button but in position to the SB, however, 95s lacks the raw equity and playability required to profitably defend against a raise.

I am on the button with 4c 9s. This hand is extremely weak, disconnected, and unsuited, making it a clear candidate for folding. There is no strategic value in playing this hand against the blinds in this spot. The standard play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.
A5o is a solid hand to open from the small blind when the action folds to me. Raising to 3x the big blind applies pressure on the big blind and gives me the initiative going into the flop.

I am in the big blind with 5-high offsuit (2d 5c), which is one of the weakest starting hands. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am the chip leader, defending with such a poor hand out of position is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
KTh is a strong suited broadway hand, making it a very solid open from the button. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to steal the blinds or build a pot with a good playable hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 8c. The Button (Pro) has opened with a 3x raise to 300. While 98o has some connectivity, playing it out of position against a 3x raise from the button in a three-handed game is generally unprofitable. Given my significant chip lead, there is no need to defend with mediocre holdings in the worst position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

T3s is a weak hand that does not play well out of position against a button opener. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting, it is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation, especially since defending the big blind with such a marginal hand often leads to difficult decisions on the flop.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c, 5d). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. My goal is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. I have a significant stack lead, and a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300) has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament. This pressure often forces the blinds to fold or play out of position against me.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (4h 3s). Facing a raise from the button (Flash), calling would be out of position with a dominated hand, and 3-betting as a bluff with such weak cards is unnecessary and risky. The most disciplined and profitable play long-term is to fold.

I have King-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x open from the Button (Flash). KTo is a decent hand that usually performs well against a wide Button opening range. While 3-betting is an option, it can bloat the pot out of position with a hand that often just makes a marginal top pair. Calling gives me a good price to see a flop and keep the pot manageable.

On a paired board of 6c 8c 8d, I completely missed the flop and have King high with no immediate draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most strategic play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here makes little sense as I have no equity to push and no strong hand to protect.

On the flop of 6c 8c 8d, I have a pocket pair of fives, giving me two pair (eights and fives). The board is paired and includes two clubs. 'Pro' has checked to me. While my fives are currently underpairs to the board, checking here could allow a free card to overcards or flush draws. A bet of around half the pot (350) serves as a continuation bet to protect my hand and extract value from smaller pairs or draws, while also gaining information on whether 'Pro' has an eight or a higher pocket pair.

I have King-high on a 6c 8c 8d board and face a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser. With no draws, no pair, and being out of position, I have very little equity to continue in this hand. Calling is unprofitable, and bluff-raising is unnecessarily risky given the coordinated nature of the board. The best decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with T7s, which is a playable suited connector for this position. Opening the betting allows me to potentially take down the blinds or play with initiative in position, which is crucial given my relatively short stack size compared to the other players.
Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the correct play from the Small Blind is to fold, as we are out of position and have a weak hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds, Five of Diamonds. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button and Pro folded. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. Suited hands like T5d have enough equity against a standard Button opening range to justify a call, especially with pot odds of better than 2:1. While out of position, the suitedness and the potential to flop a strong draw or top pair against a relatively short stack (Flashlight has 4300) make this a standard defend.

I defended the Big Blind with T5d. The flop is 6c 2d 2s, which is a fairly dry board. I have a backdoor flush draw and two overcards. Flashlight, as the pre-flop aggressor from the button, will often continue with a bet here. I should check to see how Flashlight reacts. My hand doesn't have much value right now, but I can't fold yet as I haven't seen an action.

The board is very dry, and as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to apply pressure. Even though I have just ten-high, representing a pocket pair or overcards can force a fold from my opponent who may have missed this board.

I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Five of Diamonds on a flop of 6c 2d 2s. This board is quite dry, and although I have two overcards and a backdoor diamond flush draw, my overall hand strength is very low. Flashlight, who raised from the button, has placed a continuation bet of 250. While the price to call is relatively cheap (250 into 900), I am out of position and my overcards are not guaranteed to be 'clean' outs, as I could easily be dominated by hands like AT, KT, or pocket pairs. Chasing with such a weak hand out of position often leads to difficult and unprofitable decisions on the turn and river. Therefore, I choose to fold and preserve my chip lead.

Q5o is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Even from the Button, it is not strong enough to open-raise profitably, especially facing an active small blind and a big blind who could easily shove their shorter stack. Folding is the correct decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s7s, a solid speculative hand. The button (Pro) has already folded. It is just me and Flashlight in the Big Blind. I need to put in 50 more to call, but raising is generally better to take control of the pot. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (48.5 BBs), I want to put pressure on them. A standard raise to 3x (300 total) is appropriate here to either win the pot immediately or see a flop with the initiative.

I am in the big blind with [9d, 3h], which is a very weak hand. Flash has raised from the small blind. Calling 200 to see a flop with such a dominated hand is not strategically sound, especially when playing deep in a tournament. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit. This is a very strong hand for three-handed play, especially from the button. My goal is to either win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with initiative. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate to pressure the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I'm re-raised. With a stack of 15,800, I have a significant lead and want to maintain pressure.

Flash is raising frequently from the button. My hand, A7o, is strong enough to defend here, especially given the pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450). While I am out of position, folding would be too passive against a wide button opening range. Calling is a balanced approach that allows me to see a flop and re-evaluate.

With King-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Button raise and a Small Blind call, we are out of position against two players. KTo is a hand with significant reverse implied odds, easily dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, AK, AT, or medium pocket pairs. Although we are getting decent pot odds to call, realizing that equity out of position multi-way will be difficult. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

The flop of Queen-King-Four is very dry and favors the opponent's range (Button) significantly more than mine. With only Ace-high and no real draws or backdoor equity to speak of, betting would be a pure bluff into a board that likely hit my opponent. Checking is the standard play here to see what the opponent does, and I am prepared to fold to any significant continuation bet.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a King-Queen-Four rainbow board. My hand is very strong here, especially three-handed. After Flashlight checked the flop, I should bet for value and to protect my hand from various straight draws (like J-T or J-9) and overcards. A bet of 350, which is half the pot, is a standard continuation bet that keeps my range wide while extracting value from weaker Kings, Queens, or draws.

The flop of Queen-King-Four is very dry and favors the opponent's button range significantly more than mine. With only Ace-high and no real draws or equity, I am effectively behind any pair and even many hands that might be considered bluffs in this spot. Calling 350 to win a pot of 700 with essentially zero equity on a board this disconnected is a losing play. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack rather than playing a difficult and unprofitable hand out of position.

K2 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Opening or calling with this holding would be suboptimal given the strength of the other players and the lack of playability post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

With AJo in the small blind and the button folded, I have a very strong hand for a blind vs blind encounter. I should raise to attack the big blind and build the pot. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks 3h. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind, which is a common steal attempt in a 3-handed game. My hand is marginally weak, but with a King, I have some showdown value and block some of Pro's strongest holdings. The pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 more into a 400 pot). However, out of position and with a very weak kicker, playing post-flop against a solid player like Pro is difficult. Since I want to preserve my healthy stack of 16,100 and wait for better spots, I will fold this hand and not defend with K3o against a raise.
On the button with Queen-Ten suited, which is a strong starting hand and a standard open. Raising to 3x (300 chips) is my typical sizing in this position.

I am holding Ace-Four of spades, which is a strong hand in three-handed play, especially given its nut-flush potential and the fact that I hold an Ace blocker. Pro is raising from the button, which is a wide opening range. In previous hands (#57, #60), I have successfully 3-bet Pro to 900 from the blinds, causing him to fold. I will apply the same pressure here to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop, or at least thin the field and take the initiative out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h-3h, a very weak hand. Facing an opening raise from Pro and a 3-bet from Flash, the pot odds and my hand strength do not justify a call. Folding is the only profitable and disciplined action given the strength of my opponents' ranges and my weak cards.

QcTc is a highly playable, versatile hand that performs well in position. Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, calling is the standard play. It allows me to see a flop with a hand that has excellent implied odds (straight and flush potential) and I get to evaluate the board while acting last on post-flop streets.

I 3-bet pre-flop, representing a strong range. The board [Qd 5s 6d] features a high card (Queen) that fits my perceived range better than the caller's. I will lead with a continuation bet of 700 chips (approximately 37% of the pot) to maintain the initiative and put pressure on the opponent to fold hands that missed this flop or are vulnerable, like middle pairs. If I am called, I still have an Ace as an overcard and a backdoor spade flush draw.

I flopped top pair with a decent kicker on a somewhat wet board. Facing a standard c-bet from the pre-flop 3-bettor, calling is the optimal play. It allows me to keep his bluffs (like AK, AJ, or missed broadways) in the hand while pot controlling against stronger hands in his range like AA, KK, or AQ.

The turn King of Diamonds is a double-edged sword. While it fits my perceived 3-betting range (AK, KK, KQ), it also completes a potential diamond flush and is an overcard to any Queen my opponent might be holding. I currently hold Ace-high with no diamonds and no significant draws. Since the opponent called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, they likely have a piece of the board or a draw. By checking, I am looking to control the size of the pot and potentially see a cheap river if the opponent checks back with a marginal hand like a Queen. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold as my hand has very little equity to continue against a range that called twice.

The King of diamonds on the turn is a terrible card for my hand, as it hits my opponent's 3-betting range hard (AK, KQ, KK, and flushes). Flash's check could be pot control with a better hand or giving up with air like AJ. Betting here achieves very little, as worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and evaluate the river.

The river completes a four-flush in diamonds (Qd 6d Kd Td). I hold As 4s, which has zero showdown value. My opponent called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, then checked back on the turn. This suggests they likely have a piece of the board or a marginal hand. With four diamonds on the board, any single diamond in the opponent's hand makes a flush. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the Ace of Diamonds, it is extremely risky as the opponent is likely to call with even a low diamond. Given that I have no equity, checking is the most prudent move. I will check and expect to fold if the opponent bets, or lose at showdown if they check back with a pair.

The river brings a fourth diamond to the board, making any single diamond in my opponent's hand a flush. I hold two pair (Queens and Tens) without a diamond. Since my opponent checked, I have some showdown value against complete air (like missed Ace-highs). Betting here would be disastrous, as worse hands will almost certainly fold and only better hands (flushes, higher two pairs, sets) will call. Checking behind is the only sensible option to realize any remaining showdown value and avoid losing more chips.

I have been dealt pocket Nines on the button, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table, while Flashlight is relatively short-stacked at 44 big blinds. I will open with my standard raise of 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds. Pocket Nines is strong enough to call a shove from Flashlight if it comes to that, given the stack sizes and the strength of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 53s. Facing an open raise from the button, my hand is speculative and playing out of position against a loose-aggressive opponent is difficult. With a stack of 4400, conserving chips is important and folding is the disciplined choice here.

Flash (on the Button) has opened to 300. We hold K8o in the Big Blind, which is a weak, easily dominated offsuit hand. Defending K8o out of position against a 3x raise is generally unprofitable, as it struggles to make strong hands post-flop and often suffers from kicker problems. Folding is the correct play.

I am in the best possible position (button) with a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit. Given my stack size of 4400, I need to be aggressive to chip up against the larger stacks of Pro and Flash. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard move to take control, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds or play a controlled pot in position.

87o is a weak, unsuited connector. Facing a raise from the Button while out of position in the Small Blind, and with the Big Blind still left to act, this hand does not have enough playability or equity to justify calling or 3-betting. Folding is the most profitable and standard decision.

I am holding Kh 9h in the Big Blind. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard wide opening range. Kh 9h is a very strong hand to defend with, especially given the pot odds of 2:1 on a call. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and use my hand's playability without bloating the pot out of position against a shorter stack. I will call the 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 9h. The flop is Ad Tc 5h. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has position on me. This board favors Flashlight's button opening range, as it includes many Ace-x and broadway combinations. I have one overcard (King) and a backdoor heart draw, but I do not have a piece of this board yet. Checking is the standard play for the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser to see how they react to the board texture.

I have top pair, top kicker (Ace-King on an Ace-high board). This is a strong holding and I should bet for value to get called by weaker aces, tens, or draws. Betting 400 into a 650 pot is a solid size to build the pot and extract value from my opponent's range.

The flop of Ad-Tc-5h is very favorable for Flashlight's button opening range, which contains many Ace-x and broadway hands. My hand, Kh-9h, has missed the flop entirely, offering only a marginal backdoor flush draw and a King overcard that is likely insufficient against an Ace. Flashlight's bet of 400 into a 650-chip pot represents significant strength on this texture. Given my lack of equity and the disadvantage of playing out of position against a board that hits the opponent's range so well, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
74s is a very weak hand, even from the button. While stealing is an option, it is generally better to stick to a slightly tighter range and fold hands with such low equity and playability.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The Button (Pro) has already folded. I have a strong hand for heads-up play against the Big Blind (Flashlight), who has a relatively short stack of 4650. I want to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately or play a pot with a range advantage. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is effective here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit, facing a raise from the Small Blind. This is a standard defense situation; the pot offers 2:1 odds, and K-8 is strong enough to call and see a flop against a wide SB raising range. I will call the 200 additional chips to see the flop.

I have middle pair with a strong kicker (King) on a coordinated board of Ts 9d 6h. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain initiative and protect my hand against overcards or draws. Betting 300 (half the pot) is an efficient sizing to get value from weaker hands or straight draws while keeping the pot manageable against better hands.

The flop of Ten-Nine-Six is well-connected and favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. My hand, King-Eight offsuit, has missed the board entirely and possesses no significant equity (no pair, no flush draw, and only extremely thin backdoor straight potential). With King-high as my only relative strength, I am likely behind any pair or high-card combination in the Small Blind's range. Calling a half-pot bet (300 into 900) here would be a negative expected value play, so folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a suited Queen, which is a playable hand in a three-handed game. I have a significant chip lead over both opponents. I will maintain my aggressive strategy from the button by opening with a standard raise to 300 to pressure the blinds and leverage my positional advantage.

Holding [3d, 2h], which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I am out of position in the Small Blind and facing a raise. Despite the pot odds, calling with such a weak hand is a negative expected value play. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my remaining 4400-chip stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 32 offsuit, the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, I have no equity, poor playability, and I am out of position. Folding is the only viable option.

On a very wet board of 9h-Th-7h, I have middle pair (Nines) with a gutshot straight draw. I have middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker and an open-ended straight draw (any Eight or Jack) on a very coordinated monotone board. I am out of position on the turn after calling a flop bet with middle pair and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The turn 2s is a complete brick. The board is highly coordinated with three hearts, creating significant flush and straight draw possibilities. My pair of Tens is top pair but remains vulnerable. Betting here risks bloating the pot against a range that likely includes many draws or potential made hands that have me beaten. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free card to the river, and avoid a potentially difficult check-raise situation, while keeping my opponent's range wide.

On the river, the Jack of Spades improves my hand to two pair (Jacks and Nines). By checking, I can safely get to a showdown if they check back with a Ten or a Seven, or I can evaluate a bet if they decide to bluff with a missed draw. Betting myself risks getting raised by a superior hand and likely won't get called by much worse than a Ten on such a coordinated board.

The board is highly coordinated with three hearts, creating significant flush and straight draw possibilities. 85s is a marginal, mostly weak hand. With only three players remaining, while button opening ranges are wide, 85s falls into the bottom tier of suited connectors/gappers and is typically a fold. I have King-Jack offsuit in the small blind after the button folded. This is a very strong hand for a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure on the shorter stack.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a board of 6h-Jd-3h-5s. The 5s is a relatively safe card that doesn't complete the primary flush draw (hearts). The board shows 6h-Jd-3h-5s, and I hold Ah8c, which is just Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw that did not improve. I am on the Button with Queen-Seven offsuit. While this is a marginal hand, I am the current chip leader and have been successfully applying pressure on the blinds in recent hands. We are in the Big Blind with A8o facing a button raise and a small blind call. While the pot odds are tempting (getting 3.5:1), A8 offsuit plays very poorly multiway out of position due to reverse implied odds. I am out of position in the small blind against the button. The board of Jd-9s-5c completely misses my Kh-6h, and I have no significant equity. I am the pre-flop aggressor holding Queen-high on a Jack-Nine-Five rainbow board. With the turn action of checking through, both players have demonstrated weakness on this dry board. I am first to act on the river and have essentially zero showdown value with my King-high. I am holding Queen-high on a Jack-Nine-Five-Three rainbow board. My opponent in the Small Blind has checked for the second time. I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3c, 9s). Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Flash) after a raise from the Button (Pro), the pot odds and hand strength do not justify a call. With only 2500 chips remaining, I must preserve my stack for a more favorable situation rather than committing nearly 40% of my remaining chips with 9-3 offsuit.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000, A9o is simply not strong enough to continue. Even though we have position, calling a 3-bet with an offsuit weak Ace often leaves us dominated by their value range (AJ, AQ, AK) and performs poorly post-flop. While the opponent has been somewhat active with 3-bets, 4-bet bluffing with A9o risks a significant portion of our stack unnecessarily. Folding and preserving our 94 big blinds for a more profitable spot is the best play.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have a significant chip lead, Flashlight is sitting on a short stack of less than 25 big blinds and is likely looking for opportunities to shove. There is no reason to enter the pot with such a weak hand and risk providing the short stack a double-up or facing a three-bet from the big blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Qs, 4d) and I am currently the short stack at the table (2450 chips). With the button having folded, I only need to face the big blind, but there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand OOP against a much larger stack. Folding preserves my remaining stack for a better opportunity.

Holding 4-9 offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand with no significant equity or playability. Even in late position, this hand is not worth defending or investing chips, especially with such a short stack compared to the opponents. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

With Q6o in the Small Blind and the Big Blind having a massive stack and positional advantage, this is not a strong enough hand to play or try to steal with. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve the stack.

I am on the Button with 3h 4s, which is a very weak unsuited connector. Flashlight in the Big Blind has a short stack, so raising light here to steal is risky as he might shove over the top with a wide range. The best action is to fold this trash hand.

I am the chip leader by a significant margin. Pro has folded on the button, leaving me in the small blind against a very short-stacked Flashlight in the big blind. While J7o is a hand that could be used for a blind steal, raising into a short stack of only 23.5 BBs often invites a shove that I would have to fold to, or a call that puts me out of position in a pot with a marginal hand. Given my dominant lead and the stage of the tournament, I will opt for the safest route and simply fold this junk hand to avoid unnecessary variance.

On the button in a three-handed game, King-Eight offsuit is a standard opening hand. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the Big Blind. While Flashlight is relatively short-stacked at 24.5 BBs, I have a massive chip lead and can afford to open. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to maintain pressure.

With a weak hand like Ten-Six offsuit in the Small Blind, calling a raise from the button is mathematically unfavorable and puts me at a disadvantage out of position. Given my short stack size, it is essential to conserve my remaining chips and wait for a more premium hand to make a stand.

Flash is opening very frequently from the button with a wide range. King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand that dominates many of the hands in his opening range, but it can be somewhat tricky to play out of position post-flop if I just call. By 3-betting, I can apply pressure, potentially win the pot right now, and take the betting lead if he decides to call. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing that achieves these goals.

While K8o is a standard open from the button in a three-handed game, it is not strong enough to defend against a 3-bet from the Big Blind. Pro has a stack of 85 Big Blinds and has shown a balanced 3-betting range in previous hands. Calling the 3-bet would put me in a bloated pot with a hand that is likely dominated or has poor post-flop playability. Given my substantial chip lead, there is no reason to take an unnecessary risk in a marginal spot. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent play here.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand (Ten-Three offsuit). With a short stack of only 24.5 big blinds, it is not profitable to play such a weak hand. Preserving my stack for a better opportunity is the priority.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 6s, a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. The Button has folded, and I am facing the Big Blind who has a massive chip lead. Given my poor hand and positional disadvantage, the standard play is to fold and save my chips for better spots.

J2o is a very weak hand with low equity and almost no playability post-flop. Even on the button, there is no reason to put chips in the pot with this hand. Folding is the mathematically correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 6s, a semi-connected suited hand. Pro (Button) has folded. Flashlight is in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 2350 (around 23.5 big blinds). With a suited connector in a blind-vs-blind situation, I want to apply pressure to the short stack or see a flop. Raising is generally better than calling here to take control of the hand or win the pot immediately. I'll raise to 300 to put the pressure on Flashlight.

I am the short stack at the table with only 2350 chips. Flash has raised to 300 from the small blind. With Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to defend my big blind, especially given the odds. Raising all-in might be too aggressive given my stack size, so I will call to see the flop and play from there.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind situation. The flop is Qs Qd Jc, which is quite heavy but allows me to represent a strong range including Queens, Jacks, or even AK/AJ. With 8s 6s, I have no current value, but a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard move to test my opponent's strength and potentially take the pot down immediately, especially given the short stack of Flashlight.

The board is Qs-Qd-Jc, and my Ace-high hand has completely missed this flop. Any Queen or Jack is currently beating me, and even smaller pocket pairs are ahead. Calling a bet on this paired, high-card board with only Ace-high is mathematically unfavorable, especially given that my opponent's opening range is likely connected to these high cards. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. My stack is very healthy (18,150), while Flashlight is extremely short-stacked at 2,100 (21 big blinds). Raising here is standard to put pressure on the blinds, particularly the short stack who might feel forced to shove or fold. A standard 3x open (300 total) is sufficient to accomplish this.

I am in the small blind with 8d-4d. The button (Flash) has opened for 300. My stack is very short (2100), and I am out of position against an aggressive opponent. 8-4 suited is a weak hand to defend with, especially from the small blind, as it is difficult to play post-flop without hitting a strong draw or two pair. Given the pot odds and my stack constraints, folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (Js 3c). Facing a raise from the Button, I am out of position and my hand has almost no playability or equity to justify defending. Folding is the optimal choice here to preserve my stack.

With a stack of 21 big blinds, my primary goal is to preserve my remaining chips while waiting for a high-equity hand. K-3 suited is a weak starting hand, especially in a three-handed game against two opponents with significantly larger stacks. Folding is the most prudent decision here to avoid bleeding chips in a marginal spot.

93s is too weak of a hand to play from the Small Blind, especially against the chip leader in the Big Blind who will have positional advantage post-flop. Folding is the optimal and safest decision here to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with 2d 5c, which is a very weak hand. The correct play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with a suited Jack and it has been folded to me. The Big Blind is short-stacked with only 2000 chips (20 BB). I will put pressure on the short stack by raising to 300, which is my standard opening size. This hand has decent playability if called, and I have a massive chip lead to absorb any potential losses.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Flash has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I am short-stacked (2000 chips), and calling 200 to potentially win a pot of 500 is mathematically sound, providing 2.5:1 pot odds. Defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind open is a standard play with this hand strength.

On this highly coordinated board (Ah-Ks-Kd), the range of the Big Blind (Flashlight) often hits pieces of this or is completely missed. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have a significant chip lead, I will continue with a small continuation bet. This texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range which includes many strong Ax and Kx hands. A bet of 300 (half pot) should be enough to fold out many of the opponent's weaker holdings like small pairs or random air, especially given Flashlight's short stack of 1800 chips.

I have top pair with an Ace, but with an 8 kicker, my hand is vulnerable on an Ace-King-King board. The opponent is betting 300 into a 600 pot. I have 3:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify calling with top pair. Raising here would commit too much of my short stack against a potential King or a better Ace. I will call the bet and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is the 3c, which is a complete brick. The board is Ah-Ks-Kd-3c. Flashlight, the short stack, called my flop bet and has only 1500 chips remaining in a 1200 chip pot. My Jd-5d has no showdown value. However, the board texture is extremely favorable for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. I will check here to see if Flashlight checks back, or potentially to give up if they jam, but checking allows me to realize what little equity I might have or potentially bluff the river if they show weakness. Given the stack-to-pot ratio, another bet essentially commits me or them, and since I have nothing, I'll take a free card.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board featuring two Kings. Flash checked the turn, which could be a trap or a signal that they have given up on the hand. Since the board is paired with Kings, my hand is potentially vulnerable, and checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and reach a showdown without committing more chips with a non-nut hand.

The board is Ah-Ks-Kd-3c-8d. I have Jack-high (Jd-5d) and zero showdown value. Flashlight called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet on a very high-card-heavy board. This suggests they have a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a King, or at the very least a pocket pair. Since Flashlight is extremely short-stacked (1500 chips left in a 1200 chip pot), they are unlikely to fold any pair to a river bet. Bluffing into a short stack who has already committed a significant portion of their chips is generally a losing play. I will check and hope for a miracle check-back, but I am prepared to fold if they bet.

The board is Ah, Ks, Kd, 3c, 8d. My hole cards are As and 8h. My best 5-card hand is Ah-As-Ks-Kd-8d (Two Pair, Aces and Kings). Given the board, any King beats me, and any Ace would result in a split pot (unless they have a better kicker, which is unlikely given the board texture). Flash checked the turn and river, suggesting they do not have a strong King. Betting here is likely only called by better hands (like a King) or splits with another Ace. Checking behind is the most prudent way to take this hand to showdown.

I am on the button with King-Eight suited, which is a strong opening hand in three-handed play. My current stack is the largest at the table, while Flashlight is significantly short-stacked with approximately 26 big blinds. A standard raise to 300 allows me to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. If either opponent moves all-in, I will evaluate based on the size of the jam, but K8s is robust enough to open here consistently.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak starting hand (2d, 6h). Calling the raise of 300 out of position against a player with a large stack is negative expected value. Given the low potential of these cards and my current stack size, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

We hold pocket fours in the Big Blind. Flash raises from the Button to 300. With an effective stack of over 90 BBs, we have excellent implied odds to call and set-mine against an aggressive Button opener. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have an open-ended straight draw and a small pocket pair on a 6-5-3 rainbow flop. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Checking allows me to evaluate Flash's action, and with my strong draw, I can comfortably call a continuation bet or consider a check-raise depending on the sizing.

I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a low, somewhat connected board. After Pro checked, a continuation bet of 300 (roughly half pot) is standard to take down the pot or define my opponent's range. While this board texture can hit the Big Blind's defending range, I have overcards and want to maintain the initiative I established pre-flop.

With an open-ended straight draw and an underpair on this low, coordinated 6-5-3 flop, calling the 300 continuation bet is the standard play. Our hand has excellent equity against the opponent's entire range. Calling keeps the pot manageable while allowing us to evaluate the turn and potentially hit our straight or a set.

On the turn, the 9c brings a club flush draw and doesn't improve our pocket fours or our straight draw. As the pre-flop caller and out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. We maintain our open-ended straight draw and can evaluate Flash's action, allowing us to control the size of the pot.

On the turn, the 9 of clubs is an excellent card for my hand as it gives me the nut flush draw (holding the King of clubs). Since Pro checked for the second time, I will continue with a semi-bluff. This bet of 750 (60% of the pot) maintains my initiative and puts pressure on Pro's marginal made hands like mid-pairs or weak sixes. Even if I am called, I have significant equity with 9 outs to the flush and 3 outs to a top pair with my King.

On the turn, the 9c brings a club flush draw and connects with some straight draws like 7-8, making the board much more dangerous. Flash fires a sizable second barrel of 750 into a 1250 pot. We only have an underpair and an open-ended straight draw (with potentially tainted outs if a club hits). We need about 27% equity to call profitably based on direct pot odds, but with at most 8 outs (about an 18% chance to hit on the river), we are not getting the right price. Being out of position with weak implied odds makes folding the most mathematically sound decision.

I am the short stack in this tournament, and my hand (King-Three offsuit) is too weak to warrant a call or raise against the blinds. I need to preserve my remaining 26.5 big blinds and wait for a more favorable situation or a stronger hand to commit my chips.

With Ad3d in the small blind and the button folding, this is a strong hand to open-raise. I want to either steal the big blind or build a pot with a suited ace, which plays very well post-flop. A standard raise to 300 should accomplish this.

I am holding 6h 3s in the Big Blind. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind after Flashlight folded. While this could be a wide-range steal attempt, 63 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and realizes equity poorly out of position. I have a dominant stack lead (18,300) with only 18 hands remaining in the tournament. There is no strategic reason to defend the blind with such a low-equity hand against a competent opponent like Pro.

89 offsuit is a marginal hand from the button. With a highly active big stack in the small blind and a shorter stack in the big blind who could easily shove, folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey suited after the Button has folded. Against a short-stacked Big Blind (Flashlight has about 25.5 BB), Ace-Trey suited is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take initiative in the hand. If Flashlight shoves, I have enough equity and stack depth to consider a call, but for now, a standard raise is the most effective play.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind, and I am in the big blind with Jack-Six offsuit. I have already posted 100, so it costs 200 more to call. Given the pot odds of 2:1, calling is a standard defensive play to prevent being pushed out of the blinds too easily. My hand is marginal, but it is better than folding everything that is not premium, and I should see a flop here.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board that contains a heart draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from weaker pairs (9s or 5s) and to charge any potential draws (flush or straight). Flashlight is quite short-stacked with only about 20 big blinds remaining, so I want to keep building the pot while I likely have the best hand. A half-pot bet of 300 is an appropriate size to accomplish these goals.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high and a backdoor flush draw on an Ace-high, disconnected board. Flash, the aggressor, has continued betting. My hand has very little equity against a standard opening range that hits an Ace, and calling 300 into a 900 pot with no pair and no real draw is not profitable. Folding is the correct discipline here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 4c 5c, which are low suited connectors. This is a standard opening hand from this position to apply pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is quite short-stacked (23 BB), so a raise here puts them in a tough spot, although I must be prepared to fold if they shove. I will stick to my standard open of 300 chips.

I am in the small blind with a weak suited connector (6d, 9d) and I am currently the short stack. Calling a raise out of position with this hand is not profitable given my stack size and the strength of the hand. I should preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity to shove.

K7o is too weak to defend out of position against a 3x open from the button, especially against the chip leader who can apply significant post-flop pressure. Folding is the most standard and EV-preserving play.

I am dealt pocket Kings on the button. This is a premium hand that requires aggressive play to maximize value and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot.

With Ace-Three offsuit in the Small Blind facing a button raise from the short-stacked Flashlight, calling or 3-betting is risky. Flashlight only has about 20 big blinds to start the hand and could easily shove over a 3-bet or have a better Ace. Flash is in the Big Blind with a massive stack and could squeeze. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

My hand, 7-3 offsuit, is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and offers very little post-flop playability. Flashlight is opening from the button with a short stack of 2000 chips. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1 to call the additional 200, I would be out of position against a player who is likely looking for a double-up. Given my substantial chip lead and the hand's lack of equity, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary losses.

7h5h is a marginal suited one-gapper. Although we are on the button, the Big Blind is sitting with about 23.5 big blinds, which is a prime stack size for re-stealing with a 3-bet shove. Raising here exposes us to getting jammed on, forcing us to fold and lose our open. I will fold and preserve my stack for a stronger starting hand.

I am the stack leader and the button has folded. I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (Q2o). While I could attempt to bully the short-stacked big blind with a raise, Q2o performs poorly if called or shoved on. Given my dominant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, I will choose the low-variance path and fold this marginal hand.

I am on the button with a premium hand (AKo). I have a significant chip lead over both opponents, particularly Flashlight who is down to less than 25 big blinds. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to build value and put pressure on the blinds. If either opponent shoves, I will certainly call given the strength of my hand and my stack size.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Five offsuit and a stack of 24.5 big blinds. The button has opened, and they are quite aggressive. Given the pot odds and my stack size, calling the raise is a reasonable approach to see a flop, even though I will be playing out of position. Folding feels slightly too tight given the strength of an Ace in a blind-vs-button situation.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six suited. Flash opened from the button and Flashlight called from the small blind. T6s is a very weak hand to play multi-way out of position, and the small blind is relatively short-stacked, which could mean facing an all-in later in the hand or minimal implied odds. The correct play is to fold.

I am in the small blind holding the nut flush draw (Ad) on a monotone board (3d, Td, 8d). With the nut flush draw, I have significant equity and should remain in the hand. Checking is the standard play when out of position on this texture, allowing me to evaluate the opponent's action while keeping the pot manageable, or potentially semi-bluffing later if the opportunity arises.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on a monotone flop of 3d-Td-8d. I do not hold a diamond, which makes this board very dangerous for my hand. Flashlight is a short stack with about 2200 chips remaining. While a continuation bet might take the pot down, Flashlight is unlikely to fold any pair or any diamond given their stack size. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot and get to see a turn card for free, hoping to improve to a top pair or see a non-diamond card that allows me to represent strength or get to showdown cheaply. As the chip leader, there is no need to overplay overcards on a board that favors a calling range containing many diamonds.

The turn card (4d) has completed a flush on the board, and holding the Ace of Diamonds (Ad), I have the nut flush. Since there is no betting yet on the turn, I need to lead out and build the pot for value. Checking would be too passive given I hold the absolute nuts and want to maximize my return.

The turn has brought a fourth diamond to the board, and I do not hold any diamonds in my hand. My Ace-King offsuit now has zero equity against any hand containing even the lowest diamond. Flashlight's lead for 400 into a 700-chip pot (making the total pot 1100) suggests they either have a made flush or are representing one. Since Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and I have a commanding chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a board where I am likely drawing dead. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with suited connectors (Js, 8s). This is a playable hand in late position, and since the action is folded to me, I have an opportunity to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or see a flop in position with initiative.

K7o is a weak offsuit hand, and facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind is a clear fold. Playing this hand out of position against the pre-flop raiser, with the Big Blind still left to act, is unprofitable.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4s. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. Although it is only 200 more to call into a pot of 450, my hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Flashlight is on a very short stack (2600 chips), which means any post-flop engagement will likely involve them committing a large portion of their remaining stack. Given the weakness of my hand and the stack dynamics, it is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the Button with A9o, which is a solid starting hand in this position. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard to either steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. Flashlight in the big blind has around 30 big blinds, so I need to be cautious of a 3-bet shove, but I'll fold if faced with heavy resistance.

I am holding 5s 7d in the Small Blind. Pro has raised from the button. With only 11 hands left in the tournament and a significant chip lead of 18,150, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand out of position. Additionally, Flashlight in the Big Blind is short-stacked and may decide to shove, making a call even more risky. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my dominant position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Three suited. The button has opened to 300, which is a standard raise size. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. With an Ace in hand and the suited kicker, this hand has sufficient equity to defend against a button raise. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and position.

I am in the Big Blind and have flopped the nut flush draw with Ace-high. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most profitable play is to check to him and evaluate his continuation bet. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially realize my equity or take a free card if he checks behind.

With Ace-high and no draw on a paired and two-tone board (Ts Th 7h), a continuation bet will mostly get called by better hands (any pair, any flush draw, any straight draw) and fold out hands we are already beating. Checking back allows us to realize our equity, keep the pot small, and evaluate the turn, utilizing our positional advantage.

The board is extremely paired (Ts, Th, Tc), which makes it very unlikely for either player to have a Ten if they didn't lead out on the flop. Since both players checked the flop, the likelihood of someone holding a Ten (which would make trips or quads) is diminished. I hold the Ace-high, which is a strong high-card hand on this board, but I have no made hand to value bet. Checking allows me to control the pot size, keep my opponent's range wide, and potentially realize my equity or reach a cheap showdown. A bet here would likely only be called by better hands or hands that are already crushing me, so checking is the most strategic play to preserve my stack.

The turn brings a third Ten, making the board Ts-Th-7h-Tc. My Ace-high with a 9 kicker plays as T-T-T-A-9, which is currently ahead of all non-pair hands that my opponent might hold, except for better Aces. However, any card on the river that pairs one of the opponent's hole cards will give them a full house (T-T-T-x-x), easily beating my trip Tens. Betting here serves as crucial protection to deny free equity to their 6-out unpaired combinations and charge any flush draws. A bet of roughly half-pot folds out random high-card hands and secures the pot, while allowing us to comfortably fold if faced with a check-raise.

The board is heavily paired with three Tens, which significantly reduces the likelihood that either of us has a Ten in our range, as a player with a Ten would likely have bet the flop. Pro's small bet of 300 into a 600 pot on the turn is suspicious and looks like a probing bet. Given the pot odds of 3:1 and my Ace high, which still has some showdown value and potential to improve or beat a bluff, calling is the most rational play. It is too early to fold given the small sizing, but I should be cautious on the river.

The board shows three Tens, which heavily restricts both players' ranges, as a Ten is a strong card that would have likely been bet earlier if held by either player. I have Ace-high, which is a bluff-catcher but has very little showdown value against anything other than a complete air-ball bluff. Given that I am out of position and the board is static, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot and evaluate any bet my opponent might make. Betting would only get me called by hands that beat me or cause my opponent to fold hands I currently beat, which provides no strategic advantage.

On the river, the board is T-T-T-7-2. My hand plays as trip Tens with an Ace-Nine kicker. This hand has solid showdown value against missed flush draws and random high cards like KQ or QJ that floated the turn. However, it is almost impossible to get value from worse hands, as they will fold to a bet, while any better hand (like any pocket pair, a 7, or an Ace with a better kicker) will certainly call. Attempting to bluff off a small full house is unnecessary and risky given the opponent's stack size and the pot dynamics. Therefore, checking back to get to showdown is the optimal play to realize our equity without risking more chips.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Kc Kh), the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My stack is very healthy at 18,150. Both opponents are significantly shorter than me, particularly Flashlight who has only 2,400 chips (24 big blinds). Given the strength of my hand and my position on the button, I want to build a large pot immediately. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is effective here to extract value and potentially isolate or induce a mistake from the shorter stacks.

I am in the small blind with 4-9 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Given my short stack size relative to the blinds and the aggressive nature of the button (Flash), calling would be a negative expected value play, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. I should fold to conserve my remaining chips and look for a more favorable spot.
Q3o is a very weak starting hand, making it an easy fold against a 3x open from the button, especially when out of position against the chip leader. Waiting for a better spot is the optimal play.

KsJs is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Raising on the button is the standard and most profitable play to take control of the hand, build a pot with a strong holding, and put pressure on the blinds.

With 4s 5s in the Small Blind facing a 3x open from the Button who has a short stack (about 24 BBs to start the hand), our implied odds are significantly diminished. Furthermore, the Big Blind is the chip leader and could easily squeeze if we just call. Playing a speculative hand out of position against a short stack without the correct implied odds is a losing proposition, so folding is the optimal play.

I have been dealt a premium pair of Queens in the big blind. Flashlight, who is on the button and has a relatively short stack of 2,100 (21 big blinds), has opened for 300. With the tournament approaching its final hands (141/150), and being the chip leader, I want to maximize value against a short stack's opening range. A 3-bet here is the standard play to build the pot and potentially get the opponent all-in pre-flop or on the flop. I will raise to 900, which puts significant pressure on Flashlight's remaining stack while remaining committed to calling a shove.

K-J suited is a premium holding in a 3-handed game, especially on the button. After opening, I face a 3-bet from the big blind. With a stack of 2100, calling the 600 raise is a standard and disciplined play. It keeps the pot manageable, maintains my position advantage, and allows me to evaluate the flop before committing my remaining stack of 1200 chips.

I have flopped top set with Queens on a Q-8-4 board. My opponent, Flashlight, has only 900 chips remaining in a pot of 1,850. With such a low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio), the optimal play is to put Flashlight all-in immediately. There is no benefit to checking, as I want to maximize value from any pair or draw Flashlight might be holding. A bet of 900 effectively puts the opponent all-in.

The flop of 8-4-Q is completely uncoordinated, but it heavily favors the range of an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop, as they likely have a Queen or higher pairs. My hand (K-high) has no equity (no pair, no draw). Calling the 900 bet would commit the majority of my remaining stack, leaving me with only 600 chips and no realistic chance to improve on later streets. Given the lack of a draw and the strong likelihood that I am behind, folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my remaining stack for a better spot.
97o is too weak to play from the button, especially with a short stack in the big blind who might shove, and the chip leader in the small blind. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit after the Button has folded. The Big Blind, Flashlight, is extremely short-stacked with only 14 Big Blinds remaining. Given my massive chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament (Hand 142 of 150), I want to maintain pressure on the short stacks. K2o is a standard opening hand from the Small Blind in 3-handed play. I will raise to 300 to either take the pot immediately or set up a continuation bet on the flop.

With only 14bb remaining, I am in a desperation mode where I need to maximize fold equity or realize my equity with a decent hand. [Th, 8h] is well-suited for a short-stack shove against a small blind opening range. Calling leaves me with 1100 chips and difficult post-flop decisions out of position, whereas pushing all-in puts significant pressure on Flash and gives me the best chance to double up.

Flashlight is extremely short-stacked with only 1,500 total chips (15 BB). My initial open was to 300, and Flashlight's 3-bet to 1,400 leaves him with only 100 chips behind. Pot odds dictate a call here; I need to call 1,100 more to win a total pot that will be 2,900 once he is all-in. King-high possesses sufficient equity against a wide range of hands a desperate short stack might shove with in the Big Blind. I will call the 1,400 and expect to commit the final 100 chips on any flop.

Flashlight has only 100 chips remaining in his stack after the pre-flop betting. With a pot of 2800, the pot odds are overwhelming for both players. I have King-high on a highly connected board (7-6-8), which is not ideal, but given that he has less than one big blind left, I will bet 100 to put him all-in and proceed to the showdown.

I am facing a bet of 100 into a 2800 pot, which offers me incredible pot odds. With a pair of eights and a flush draw on a board of 7h-6h-8c, my hand has significant equity. Given that I have only 100 chips remaining, this is an automatic call to see the rest of the board.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant advantage. Holding Queen-Five of diamonds on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening range hand to attempt a steal. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds, particularly Flashlight who is the short stack. If either opponent shows significant resistance, I can easily fold and maintain my lead.

Holding Kh5s out of position against a button raise is weak. Given my short stack of 2950 and the positional disadvantage, I cannot afford to invest chips with a marginal hand. Folding allows me to wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining stack.

Flash is opening to 3x from the button, which is standard for them and implies a wide range, but Q4 suited is still too weak to defend from the Big Blind out of position. I'll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

King-deuce offsuit is a weak hand with very little playability, especially at this late stage of the tournament. With 29.5 big blinds, I should prioritize higher equity hands and avoid unnecessary risks with marginal cards, even in position on the button.

With 98o in the Small Blind against the aggressive, big-stacked Big Blind, folding is the most prudent play. We are out of position with a marginal offsuit connector, and the Big Blind will frequently raise or put pressure on us post-flop. Preserving our stack of 9,100 is better than engaging in a disadvantageous spot.

With J9o on the button, this hand is generally too weak to open-raise, especially with the Big Blind sitting on a sub-30 bb stack that could easily 3-bet jam over a steal attempt, and the Small Blind holding a massive chip lead. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am the chip leader with 179 BBs, and the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 145 of 150). The button has folded, leaving me in the small blind against the short-stacked big blind (Flashlight, ~28 BBs). My hand, Ten-Five offsuit, is very weak.
