There is no reason to take unnecessary risks or provide the short stack with an opportunity to double up. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my massive lead.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. I have the chip lead, and both opponents have significantly smaller stacks, particularly Flashlight in the small blind. Raising to 300 is a standard open that applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to take control of the pot early. Given that there are only five hands remaining in the tournament, I want to maintain my lead by picking up blinds and antes where possible without taking extreme risks.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Queen-Three offsuit). Calling a raise out of position with this hand is poor value, especially with a short stack where I need to conserve chips for a better opportunity. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am holding Ace-King suited (Ah Kh) in the Big Blind facing a standard 3bb open from the Button, who has been very active and aggressive. This is a premium hand, and 3-betting is the optimal play to extract value, take the betting lead, and punish a potentially wide button opening range. A 3-bet size around 3.5x out of position is standard.

I opened with Ace-Six offsuit from the button, which is a standard attempt to steal the blinds given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament. However, Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the big blind. While I am getting decent pot odds (700 to call into a 1350 pot) and would have post-flop position, A6o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by Pro's 3-betting range (typically 77+, AJ+, or strong broadways). With only five hands left and a massive lead of over 17,000 chips compared to Pro's 8,100, there is no reason to take an unnecessary risk or play a large pot with a marginal hand. I will fold to preserve my stack and maintain my dominant position for the final few hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. With only a few hands left in the tournament and a short stack of 2950, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that allows me to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet.

With Q3s in the Small Blind facing a Button open, my hand is too weak to call or 3-bet, especially with the Big Blind still left to act and the Button being somewhat short-stacked. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Big Blind with As7h. Flashlight on the Button has raised to 300. With the Small Blind folding, I am getting 2.25-to-1 odds to call (200 more into a pot of 450). As7h is a solid hand to defend against a wide button opening range. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack of 2650, I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate my position post-flop, as 3-betting might invite a shove from a range that dominates mine.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of Ac-8s-7c. This is a very strong hand. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has a relatively small stack of 2,650 remaining. I will check to allow them the opportunity to continuation bet with their range, which includes many hands I dominate. If they bet, I can then decide between calling to keep their bluffs in or raising to commit them to the pot.

I have top pair with an Ace on a slightly coordinated board with a potential flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to gain value from weaker holdings and to force out drawing hands. Betting roughly half the pot (300 chips) is an appropriate size to build the pot while protecting my hand.

I have flopped a very strong two-pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of Ac-8s-7c. My opponent, Flashlight, is the pre-flop aggressor and is playing a relatively short stack of approximately 23.5 big blinds. While calling could induce further bluffs, the board has several draws, including flush draws and straight draws (like 9-10 or 6-5), that I do not want to give a free card to. By raising to 900, I am building the pot to maximize value and setting up an easy commitment of the remaining chips on the turn, especially since any Ace or strong draw in the opponent's range is unlikely to fold.

The opponent's check-raise on an Ace-high board is aggressive, but with my current stack size, folding top pair is too passive given the pot odds (3:1). The board is not overwhelmingly dangerous, and the opponent may be attempting to represent a stronger hand or semi-bluffing with a draw. I am priced in to call and re-evaluate on the turn, as committing a significant portion of my stack with top pair is the most rational play against this wide range of possibilities.

I have a strong two-pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of Ac-8s-7c-4c. The 4c completes potential club flushes, but the stack-to-pot ratio is very low (Flashlight has 1,750 chips left with a 2,450 chip pot). Given my opponent's commitment after calling the flop check-raise, it is unlikely they will fold any Ace or significant draw. I will bet 1,750 to put Flashlight all-in, maximizing value from worse Aces and protecting my hand against single-club draws or straight draws that might take a free card if I check.

With only 1750 chips remaining and the pot already at 4200, I am effectively priced into this call. My top pair, while vulnerable to sets or two-pair, has significant equity against the many semi-bluffs and draws (like flush draws) that Flash might be playing aggressively on this wet board. Given this is the 147th hand of 150, folding would leave me with an extremely short stack, essentially ending my chances of competing. I must call to maximize my potential to double up and stay in the tournament.

With King-Ten suited on the button in a heads-up scenario, I have a very strong starting hand. I want to raise to take control of the hand, build the pot, and put pressure on the big blind. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o. The player 'Pro' on the Button has raised to 300. With only three hands left in the tournament and a substantial chip lead over Pro, K8o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay Pro post-flop without committing too many chips to a pre-flop confrontation.

On a paired board of Ad Ah 9c, I am out of position as the Big Blind. King-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace or Nine in the opponent's range. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here, especially with a significant chip lead and only a few hands remaining in the tournament. I will check and evaluate based on Pro's action.

On a dry, paired board like Ad-Ah-9c, my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser is very strong and includes many Aces. Flash checking to me allows me to make a standard continuation bet to fold out better high cards or small pocket pairs. A small sizing like 1/3 pot is highly effective here to apply pressure while risking the minimum.

On this dry, paired board of Ad-Ah-9c, the opponent's small continuation bet of 200 into a 600-chip pot is a standard play that can be made with a wide range, including many hands my King-high currently beats. Given the 4:1 pot odds and the fact that I have a significant chip lead with only three hands remaining in the tournament, calling to see a turn is a reasonable defense. I am not yet ready to concede the pot to a single small bet on such a static texture.

On the turn, the 8h has improved my hand to two pair, Aces and Eights with a King kicker. However, the board remains dangerous as any Ace in my opponent's range has me crushed. As the first to act out of position, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to evaluate 'Pro's' reaction and prevents the pot from ballooning unnecessarily, which is vital given my dominant chip lead and the fact that there are only three hands remaining in the tournament. I am looking to reach showdown cheaply or catch a bluff.

My opponent called my continuation bet on an Ace-paired board, indicating they likely have a piece like a 9, a pocket pair, or maybe even a slow-played Ace. With King-high, no pair, and no draw on the turn, I have very little equity and limited fold equity against the calling range. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size and take a free card to the river.

On the river with a board of Ad-Ah-9c-8h-6h, I have two pair, Aces and Eights, with a King kicker. The 'Pro' player on the Button checked back the turn, which significantly reduces the likelihood they hold an Ace. Given my substantial chip lead and the fact that there are only two hands remaining after this one, my primary goal is to minimize risk. By checking, I can safely reach showdown. If Pro bets, I can evaluate based on the size, but checking is the most prudent way to realize my hand's value without unnecessarily bloating the pot or opening myself up to a raise.

On the river, the board completes a potential backdoor flush and some straight draws. I only have King high and no piece of this board. My opponent called the flop c-bet, meaning they likely have at least a small pair, a 9, or an Ace. Because I block some of the missed broadway floats (like KT, QT, JT) that would fold to a bet, my opponent's range is weighted more heavily toward pairs that might call a bluff. Checking behind is the most conservative and optimal play to avoid risking more chips, occasionally winning at showdown against a missed draw.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Ten-Eight offsuit. This is a strong hand in a 1-on-1 match, well within the range of hands that should be played aggressively. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the Big Blind.

A6o is a reasonable hand to defend in the big blind heads-up. While the 3x raise makes it slightly marginal to call out of position, having an Ace blocker and high-card equity justifies seeing a flop. I will call and evaluate post-flop.

I flopped two pair, Aces and Sixes, on a fairly wet board that includes a spade flush draw. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard to induce a continuation bet. I plan to check-raise to build the pot for value and to protect my hand against potential draws.

I have middle pair (Eights) on an Ace-high board. While my hand has some value, betting into an Ace-high board in a heads-up pot often results in being called by better (an Ace) or folding out worse. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to potentially see a cheap showdown or catch bluffs on later streets. It also protects my checking range, making it harder for my opponent to put me on a specific hand strength.

With top and bottom pair (Aces and Sixes) on a board with flush and straight draws, my hand is very strong. Since Flash checked back the flop, their range is likely capped at weak Aces, marginal pairs, or draws that took a free card. The 2c on the turn is a blank. It's time to start building the pot for value and to charge any draws. A bet of 400 (two-thirds pot) accomplishes both goals.

I have middle pair (Eights) on an Ace-high board. After checking back the flop to control the pot, my opponent has led out on the turn with a 400 bet into a 600 pot. My hand is too strong to fold in a heads-up situation against a wide range, as Pro could be betting a draw or a weaker pair (like a 6). However, raising is unnecessary since it would likely only be called by an Ace or better. Calling is the most profitable line to keep the pot controlled and see what the river brings.

On the river, the 3d is a relatively blank card, and my two pair (Aces and Sixes) remains very strong. The flush draws missed, and it's highly unlikely Flash backed into a straight with 45. Flash's turn call strongly points to a weak or medium Ace, or a stubborn middle pair like an 8. I need to bet for value to extract chips from those single-pair hands. A bet of around two-thirds pot should be the right price to get called by hands like AT, A9, or A7.

I have middle pair (Eights) on an Ace-high board. By checking back the flop, I kept the pot small and protected my range, but it also signaled that I likely do not have an Ace. Pro has capitalized on this by leading the turn and river. In a heads-up context, an Eight is a strong bluff-catcher against missed spade draws or straight draws (like 9-7 or 7-5). The river bet of 1,000 into a 1,400 pot gives me roughly 2.4-to-1 odds, meaning I only need to be right about 30% of the time to break even. Given that this is a heads-up match and my hand is near the top of my range for this line, I will call to catch potential bluffs.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am dealt Queen-Eight offsuit, which is a marginal hand even in a heads-up scenario. Given my goal is to maximize my stack size and end the tournament securely, taking on unnecessary variance against an aggressive opponent with a mediocre holding is not worth the risk. I will simply fold and preserve my stack.
